attributes.stem: A previously well 65-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department following a fall. A hip/pelvis X-ray shows a complete fracture line through the junction of the head and neck of femur with partial displacement. As part of her management after hip surgery she will require some form of prophylaxis against venous thromboembolism.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be most appropriate?
attributes.explanation:
Venous thromboembolism is a serious, yet preventable complication during and after hospitalisation. Without prophylaxis, the incidence of VTE in medical and general surgical patients ranges from 10-40% and 40-60% in major orthopaedic surgery patients. Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH), such as enoxaparin is the preferred option for venous thromboembolism prophylaxis. 40mg is the standard dose, given no renal impairment.

All adult orthopaedic patients undergoing total hip arthroplasty and total knee arthroplasty require thromboprophylaxis. Total hip arthroplasty, as for this patient, requires up to 35 days of LMWH post surgery. This is due to the risk of late-occurring DVT following total hip arthroplasty remaining high until at least day-35 post surgery.

Unfractionated heparin is an option for patients who have renal impairment and those at an increased of bleeding. Other options, particularly for total hip arthroplasty are fondaparinux, direct oral anticoagulants (DOAC), specifically rivaroxaban, and dabigatran etexilate. While fondaparinux and rivaroxaban both significantly reduce DVT risk compared to LMWH, fondaparinux is associated with more bleeding. Hence, fondaparinux is not the first line choice, particularly in the elderly, those with renal impairment, in the frail and those weighing less than 50kg. When compared to DOAC, LMWH has a shorter half life and has less profound bleeding which is important during the peri-operative period for intra-op bleeding and for post-op wound healing. Additionally, post-marketing surveillance on adverse events for rivaroxaban and dabigatran etexilate are not yet available and therefore, these are used with caution.
learningPoints: Major surgical procedures will usually require a period of anticoagulation for at least 14 days after discharge from hospital.
Pharmacological thromboprophylaxis for total hip arthroplasty has been recommended for up to 35 days following surgery
LMWH, fondaparinux, rivaroxaban & dabigatran etexilate can be used as pharmacological thromboprophylaxis for total hip arthroplasty in the absence of contraindications
references: Flevas DA, Megaloikonomos PD, Dimopoulos L, Mitsiokapa E, Koulouvaris P, Mavrogenis AF. Thromboembolism prophylaxis in orthopaedics: an update. EFORT Open Rev. 2018;3(4):136-48.
Lieberman JR, Heckmann N. Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis in Total Hip Arthroplasty and Total Knee Arthroplasty Patients: From Guidelines to Practice. J Am Acad Orthop Surg. 2017;25(12):789-98.

Safer Care Victoria. Victorian Guideline for the Prevention of Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) in Adult Hospitalised Patients. 2023. https://www.safercare.vic.gov.au/sites/default/files/2023-10/Guideline%20for%20the%20Prevention%20of%20Venous%20Thromboembolism.pdf
